We were discussing the second pillar of fasting which is refraining from anything that would break a person's fast. And then the author lists those things that will break a person's fast. The author cites six things as being the six things that will compromise a person's fast. Meaning that these six things will make it so that if a person does them purposely, they must make that day up again. And so the first and second was eating and drinking purposely. Eating and drinking purposely. And the scholars have explained what is meant by eating or drinking. And that is that anything foreign goes down the throat. Anything foreign that goes down the throat, whether it be by way of mouth or by way of the nose. not the eyes and not the ears. These are not considered direct paths to the throat. The direct paths to the throat are the mouth and the nose. And we mentioned that this has to be something that is done purposely meaning a person knows that they're fasting. and remember at the moment and they proceed to then knowingly eat or drink anything that will compromise their fast. This will break a person's fast. Now, it also warrants mentioning that anything that would take the place of food and drink, even if it is not by way of the throat, even if it is not by way of the mouth and nose. What do we mean by that? Well, let's say a person were to use an IV drip, right? This is something that is used to nourish the body in place of food and drink or let's say a bag that were to go or a tube that were to go directly into the stomach, right? And a person were to take nourishment through that, this will count as eating and drinking, right? If a person's in such a state that they need to have IV or they need to have you know what do they call this the feeding tube right or going in through the throat or anything into the stomach then this person is beyond ill. This person doesn't need to be fasting in the first place. However, if a person were to use this, this would break a person's fast. Why? Because the spirit of the commandment is eating and drinking. And the spirit of the prohibition is that a person refrain from that which nourishes the body by way of eating and drinking or anything that takes food or drinks place in that sense. This will break a person's fast. However, if it is not designed to take the place of food and drink, for instance, a vitamin shot, right? or insulin or you know any of these kinds of things that are not designed to take the place of food and drink but they are a kind of vitamin or a kind of nourishment but not a nourishment that takes the place of food and drink. This will not break a person's fast. Is that a clear distinction in your mind? So there are things that nourish the body a a B12 shot, vitamins, right? Or things that are used as a partial nourishment. or for instance for diabetes, insulin, right, which is produced in the body, but it is a reaction to eating and drinking or for instance something that is partially designed to nourish the body. This will not break a person's fast. What will break a person's fast is taking something that is designed to substitute food and drink. This will break a person's fast. Why? Well, it may not go down the throat, but it is in direct conflict with the spirit of the prohibition. The prophet sallall.aii Wasallam said that Allah himself said regarding the person who fasts. He said that Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala speaks of the person fasting and says that the person fasting leaves off his food and drink and sexual gratification for my sake. So the spirit of fasting is exactly that narration. The narration is in Bkari is exactly captured in that narration which is leaving off food and drink and sexual gratification. This is the spirit of fasting. And so taking something, introducing something into the body that will take the place of food and drink is in direct conflict with the spirit of fasting and the purpose of fasting. Therefore, it will break a person's fast. The author then cites the evidence. We mentioned that evidence is the difference between knowledge and right which is to just follow right to when we say follow we could actually call imitate to imitate right the scholars agree that imitation is not considered knowledge this is an issue of consensus Abd in his book on knowledge mentions that the scholars all agree and have reached consensus that an imitator is not a scholar is not a person of knowledge. Let's say we had a person that memorized an entire memorized volumes of books with respect to the opinions of the scholars of Islam, right? And any subject that you mention, they will cite to you all four m this med this is the opinion. This m this is the opinion. This MB, this is the opinion. But if they are asked what the evidence is, evidence is Quran, Sunnah, the and that which is associated with Quran and Sunnah. So Quran, Sunnah, the words of the companions, the fatawa of the companions, right? So if if this person were to be asked about the evidences for this, they don't know the evidences. They only know what the final opinions of the scholars are. This is not a person of knowledge. This may be a person of knowledge with respect to the opinions of the scholars. He knows what the scholars said, but he doesn't know why they said it. And that is what knowledge is. Knowledge is knowing that which is correct with its evidence with its evidence from the Quran and Sunnah. So this is why we mention the evidence and you know it's really sad sometimes when you see the general attitude of a lot of people with respect to the evidence right when you go to I mean even myself people will ask me a question a lot of times they'll ask a question and I'll begin with mentioning the answer and then citing the evidence they tune in for the answer and then their eyes glaze is over when I mention the evidence I say the answer to this question is you know the following because Allah said in this verse and their eyes just glaze over and they zone out they check out as if to say well that doesn't really concern me what concerns me is just the ev the the final conclusion and you know anything beyond that is the dominion of you know the domain of scholars. summary: This lecture discusses the pillars of fasting in Islam, specifically focusing on the second pillar which involves refraining from actions that break the fast. The speaker explains the six things that invalidate a fast, with emphasis on eating and drinking. The lecture covers the definition of eating and drinking (anything foreign going down the throat through mouth or nose), distinguishes between substances that substitute for food/drink versus those that merely provide nourishment (like vitamins or insulin), and provides Quranic and Hadith evidence for these rulings. A significant portion addresses the importance of understanding evidence from the Quran and Sunnah rather than merely following scholarly opinions without comprehension.
suggested_title: The Pillars of Fasting: What Breaks the Fast and the Importance of Understanding Evidence
catchy_title: Six Things That Break Your Fast
